text: We describe a technique of robotic low anterior resection in a patient who developed regrowth following a complete clinical response for acute cancer. A seventy-three-year-old lady with T-for-N-zero tumor developed a complete response following long-course chemovariation. During wait-and-watch policy, unfortunately, she became symptomatic and a second three-monthly scan suggests dependence of regrowth. The scan on the left shows complete clinical response with no residual tumor, while the scan on the right side now suggests presence of luminal regrowth with threatened margin as shown by this arrow.

The surgical team performs a low anterior dissection, beginning with the ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) near their origins. The medial lateral plane is developed, staying anterior to the uterine fascia. The splenic flexure is mobilized, and attention shifts towards pelvic dissection to achieve total mesorectal excision (TME).

The surgeon performs posterior dissection between the hypogastric nerves, using robotic instruments to precisely mobilize the rectum deep in the pelvis. The hypogastric nerves are clearly visible, and care is taken to avoid damaging them during the dissection.

The surgeon uses gentle traction with the robotic second hand at the Crouch of Douglas level, a centimeter or two above the posterior wall of the vagina, to progress the dissection anteriorly. Counter-traction provided by the second robotic left hand aids in precise resection behind the back wall of the vagina, before further dissection on each side of the rectum.

The surgeon performs an extra-fascial dissection on the posterior rectal side, leaving a piece of pre-sacral fascia intact with the specimen to facilitate an R-zero resection. This decision is informed by pre-operative MRI imaging indicating a threatened circumferential resection margin (CRM).

The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing the endopelvic fascia surrounding the fully mobilized rectal tube. The rectum is divided using a 45mm endo-GIA stapler with two firings. The empty pelvis is visible, showing intact pre-sacral nerves and fascia. The specimen is extracted through the left liposa, and a side-to-end anastomosis is performed. A loop ileostomy is placed in the right leg force as a defunctional stoma.
